data.title: Lyft: The AV Dark Horse With Breakout Potential
data.description:
Introduction:

Lyft (LYFT) has seemingly lived in the shadow of its rival Uber Technologies (UBER) for as long as the two firms have existed. However, I believe that Lyft is positioned to blossom as the adoption of autonomous vehicles becomes mainstream.

Lyft has been building a vertically integrated, scalable business that could create substantial shareholder value if they are able to capture additional market share and forge partnerships with other companies in the autonomous driving space.

In this article, I will cover Lyft's business model, recent operating results, and the catalysts and risks that will define the company's path moving forward. Finally, I've created a discounted cash flow model that includes my assumptions regarding the financial profile of the business over the upcoming decade.

Business Description & Financials:

Lyft is a key player in the transportation industry, as they operate one of the largest multimodal networks in North America. Lyft's core business remains centered around connecting riders with drivers, although they have expanded into bike rentals, car leasing services for drivers on their platform, and a number of other complementary revenue streams.

The company reported solid operating results in Q1 FY25, with active riders increasing by 11% YoY to 24.2 million and total rides growing by over 16% to 218.4 million. This reflects both user growth and higher engagement from each individual on the platform.

Gross bookings, which represents the dollar amount riders spent on the platform, saw a marked increase of 13% YoY to $4.2 billion. This result is even more impressive when considering the fact that the company has been aggressively expanding operations in lower cost of living markets across Canada, as well as smaller U.S. cities like Indianapolis and Charlotte. These regions all saw solid growth, however the average ride is lower in price due to lower median income levels (which partially negates the positive impact on the gross bookings metric).

Total revenue this past quarter increased a respectable 14% YoY to $1.45 billion, and the firm's operating loss narrowed to just $28.8 million compared to $63 million during the year ago period. Lyft also achieved a net profit of $2.6 million, which more than doubled compared to Q1 FY24. The discrepancy between operating and net income was attributable to $33.4 million in net interest income during the most recent period.

Both adjusted EBITDA and FCF grew rapidly, up 79% and 121% YoY, respectively. The drastic improvement in Lyft's profitability profile has allowed the firm to expand its share buyback program to a whopping $750 million. This also indicates the management team's confidence in the long-term growth outlook of the company, and it hints at the fact that they believe shares are presently being undervalued by the market.

The company recently announced the launch of Lyft Silver which is a version of the platform that caters to senior citizens in the U.S. market. Lyft is offering this cohort of consumers a simplified user interface as well as the ability to order vehicles that are more accessible for those with disabilities. Now is a fantastic time to target this demographic, given that the number of senior citizens in the U.S. is projected to reach 70 million by 2030.

Lyft also recently acquired the preeminent European taxi platform FREENOW, marking the company's first move into developed international markets. The integration of the FREENOW platform will give Lyft access to a total of ~50 million riders across the planet, which should allow the company to compete more effectively against their primary nemesis, Uber. Lyft is also allowing consumers to order traditional taxis through its platform in St. Louis, which is another move that aims to bring the Lyft platform up to speed as Uber has been offering traditional taxis in select markets for a while now.

Autonomous:

We are living through a massive paradigm shift in the ride hailing industry, driven by the rise of autonomous driving technology. So far, it appears that Uber has taken a commanding lead over Lyft in the autonomous vehicle [AV] arms race, as Uber has partnered with numerous companies that create the technology layer powering self-driving cars. Uber has inked contracts with companies such as Lucid, Waymo, Momenta, Baidu, Pony.ai, WeRide, and others to offer self driving vehicle services across the globe through its platform.

While Uber appears to be focused on scaling quickly and creating brand association with autonomous driving, I believe that Lyft is building a more durable and monetizable business model. Similar to Uber, Lyft is focusing on being the demand aggregation platform that connects riders, AV fleet owners, and tech providers. However, a key differentiating factor between the two competing firms is Lyft's wholly owned subsidiary, Flexdrive.

Flexdrive focuses on fleet management, and the company currently manages over 15,000 vehicles at an average utilization rate of 90%. It will be absolutely crucial for AV fleet operators to maintain maximum uptime in order to earn reasonable returns on their investments, especially over the near term given the high costs associated with manufacturing safe AVs.

Flexdrive will provide fleet operators with daily vehicle preparation services, predictive maintenance solutions, charging optimization, and other tools that will ensure customers' vehicles stay on the road as consistently as possible. This drives a competitive advantage as Uber does not currently have a comparable vertically integrated fleet management subsidiary.

I personally believe that Lyft and Flexdrive as a tandem, will be able to offer AV fleet operators significantly better unit economics than Uber by bundling their respective offerings. Instead of paying a ridesharing platform and a fleet management company separate checks, AV operators can partner with Lyft and get immediate access to both of these essential services.

In my view, this will be especially appealing to smaller fleet operators, who likely won't have the scale or the relationships necessary to negotiate competitive pricing with both rideshare platforms and car servicers. I also personally believe that the future of autonomous driving will be relatively democratized, with everyday consumers being able to purchase vehicles that are equipped with autonomous driving technology out of the box.

Lyft CEO David Risher corroborated my view on the latest earnings call, as he envisions a world in which vehicles are designed to be "Lyft ready," meaning they come equipped with autonomous driving technology and can generate passive income on the Lyft platform upon purchase.

Circling back to the technology layer, Lyft has also agreed to a number of partnerships that support their efforts in the autonomous driving space. In an announcement made late last year, Lyft outlined separate agreements with three companies in the industry: Mobileye, May Mobility, and Nexar. I view the Mobileye partnership as the most strategically important, as any vehicle that is equipped with Mobileye's autonomous driving solution will eventually be able to operate on the Lyft platform. Lyft will then handle the backend operations, such as booking rides, route optimization, and pricing, in exchange for a cut of the revenue generated by these vehicles.

The Nexar partnership is also brilliant, as Nexar produces smart dashcams that have collected vast amounts of road level video and data. This can be used by Lyft's future partners to train effective, regulatory friendly autonomous driving algorithms. Uber, despite a significant number of partnerships, doesn't have access to a dataset akin to Nexar's.

Another interesting aspect of the autonomous driving landscape is insurance. Today, both Lyft and Uber incur significant expenses related to insurance in order to operate their respective platforms. However, as we move toward Level 5 full automation, I believe that the liability will increasingly shift towards software providers.

If a robotaxi causes an accident due to an issue in the algorithm, the responsibility should fall on the software developer instead of the platform operator. With that being said, Lyft currently provides insurance coverage even when their drivers are the ones at fault, so it is likely that platform operators will still bear a portion of the insurance costs.

Regardless of how the insurance burden is split, it is likely that insurance costs will be reduced over time. Goldman Sachs analysts currently project that AV insurance costs per mile could fall by over 50% over the next 15 years given the lower accident rates of self-driving cars.

This will act as a tailwind for the autonomous driving industry as a whole, allowing more new entrants and in turn a greater level of supply on the Lyft platform. This will create a virtuous cycle as more supply will lead to lower prices, which will further increase demand from consumers.

And finally, while Uber is certainly in the lead in regards to deploying autonomous vehicles today, I believe there is more than enough room for both companies to achieve massive levels of success. Management commented on this idea recently, essentially stating that the first mover advantage in autonomous driving is overstated. They believe that AV tech providers, OEMs, and fleet operators will choose to partner with multiple ride hailing platforms to avoid vendor lock in, which I completely agree with.

Take the cloud industry for example, as Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) have all built substantial business lines and created hundreds of billions of dollars in shareholder value through their competing offerings. I believe autonomous driving will follow a similar trajectory, as there is more than enough room for multiple winners given that the size of this market is estimated to reach over $1.8 trillion in the coming years.

Overall, Lyft appears to be building a very practical and scalable business model that is positioned well to capitalize on this transformational shift in the transportation industry. Over time, I view Lyft as a strong contender in the space, as they will be able to effectively serve a growing number of small AV fleet owners who will need access to high-quality demand and servicing infrastructure.

Risks:

There are a couple of risks at the moment that investors must consider before initiating a position in Lyft. First off, Lyft's insurance coverage remains a significant expense that inhibits the company's ability to generate substantial profits. Their coverage for drivers ranges from $125K to $1 million depending on various different factors, which weigh heavily on gross margins. Additionally, according to third party reports, insurance costs for ridesharing companies are roughly 12% higher than the costs of insuring regular drivers.

Competition from Uber is also another factor at play, given that Lyft is currently a significantly smaller player both domestically and internationally. Lyft recently lost its partnership with Delta Airlines to its primary competitor, which I believe introduces a larger concern that corporations simply view Uber as a superior platform at the present moment. If this perception doesn't change over the coming years, Lyft may struggle to secure as many large-scale AV partnerships, which will hamper the long-term growth story.

I also see issues related to Lyft's international growth strategy. Management has explicitly stated that they are solely focused on shoring up operations related to the FREENOW acquisition in a select few European markets, and that they are not currently looking at further expansion.

While I appreciate the company's commitment to improving the rider experience in certain designated areas, I also believe that their cautious attitude towards expanding into new geographies will limit their TAM over the long run. Now is a perfect time to begin introducing the Lyft app overseas, especially if Lyft were able to sign new agreements with AV tech providers to begin offering autonomous vehicles in countries where it is not prohibited.

Valuation:

To determine Lyft's intrinsic value, I created a discounted cash flow model. The perpetual growth rate I used in the model was 2%, and my projection spans the upcoming decade.

For my cost of capital, I first computed the cost of debt using the TTM interest expense and the debt and tax rate information available online. Next, I calculated the cost of equity using the yield on a 10-year U.S. Treasury bond as my risk free rate, the stocks beta listed on S&P Capital IQ, and the equity risk premium from Professor Aswath Damodaran's website.

Below are my calculations for both the weighted average cost of capital [WACC] and my discounted cash flow model inputs:

For the revenue growth rates, I assume that the rise in popularity of autonomous vehicles will lead to lower operating costs, which stands to benefit consumers in the form of lower rider fares. This should, in time, increase demand for ridesharing services overall.

Additionally, this report highlights how the ride hailing service market is projected to grow at a 15.1% CAGR over the next decade, which further supports my estimated revenue growth rates.

For operating margins during the projection period, I assumed that AV adoption will reduce the payouts made to drivers (as Lyft currently pays out around 70% of gross bookings to its drivers).

While I do believe there will still be human drivers over the entirety of the upcoming decade, I also think that AVs will likely grow to at least 40% of total rides by the end of my projection period. This should therefore reduce the company's operating expense burden and lead to margin expansion.

Another reason I have Lyft's margins set to expand is that insurance costs are likely to decline substantially over the next couple of years as more research becomes published related to the increased safety of autonomous vehicles.

Moving along to the cash flow statement items, I decided to keep D&A and Capex roughly in line with Lyft's historical averages, as I believe the company will still have to invest in developing the backend infrastructure that powers the Lyft platform, along with the necessary capital expenditures related to building out the Flexdrive platform to support Lyft's AV fleet operator partners.

For the change in net working capital [CNWC] estimates, I assumed that as the company transitions towards an autonomous vehicle model, its operations will become far more streamlined. This should mean that less cash is tied up in operations, which is why I modeled a gradual decline in this figure as a percentage of revenue.

Overall, my model implies a fair value of $33.31 per share, which represents ~125% upside from current share price levels. While some of my assumptions may be viewed as too optimistic, I believe that my projections are absolutely within the realm of possibility if the company executes well.

Conclusion:

Although Lyft faces intense competition and some uncertainty around its expansion plans, I believe the company's deliberate and focused approach to autonomous vehicle integration creates a setup for substantial share price appreciation.

My DCF model includes optimistic assumptions; however, I believe these could end up looking conservative a few years from now if regulatory hurdles are overcome and AV adoption accelerates. While investors should be aware of the downside risks here, I believe this stock has an asymmetric risk reward profile, and therefore I am rating shares of Lyft as a strong buy.

data.title: Coursera Q2: A Great Buy As Growth Accelerates
data.description:
As the S&P 500 continues to try to find solid ground at fresh all-time highs, my strategy continues to be to rotate out of winning momentum trades and into more overlooked small-cap stocks that have a better valuation appeal. My macro view is that the markets will trade relatively flat (or even slightly decline) through the remainder of 2025, but that lagging small caps will catch up to their large cap peers.

Coursera (NYSE:COUR) is a powerful example of the gains that can be unlocked in underappreciated small caps that deliver strong earnings results. The online learning company just reported a strong beat-and-raise quarter in Q2, sending its stock up more than 20% to the ~$11 levels in after-hours trading. In my view, it's a great time for investors to reassess the bull case in Coursera as the company's growth rates accelerate.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Coursera in April, when the stock was still trading in the mid-$8s. Since then, Coursera's post-earnings rally has made it one of the strongest winners in my small-cap portfolio, but considering the company's elevated growth trajectory as well as its significant improvement in free cash flow generation, I continue to see a long runway ahead for this stock and am reiterating my buy opinion here.

For investors who are newer to Coursera, here is what I view as the long-term bull case in this name:

  • Highly scalable business mode that can be tailored to millions of prospective students. Online for-profit education is an industry that many view with skepticism, especially since the space has been littered with failures. But where fallen rivals like 2U have failed is in taking on custom course development costs for each individual university it partners with. Coursera, by contrast, offers its platform as a service without taking responsibility for course development, and its courses are broadly applicable even beyond a degree context.
  • AI course growth. The company recently surpassed 10 million total enrollees in its generative AI courses and certifications, with the company noting that it signs up a new enrollee every 12 minutes.
  • AI machine language translation for its own course content. The company began in 2023 to utilize AI to translate text and voice content into dozens of foreign languages, opening the door to immense international growth opportunities. It will next use AI to dub course content into different languages as well.
  • Growing appeal of certificates, especially in AI. There are new skills, such as AI, that are in high demand and not necessarily subjects that were available for specialization in college. Coursera is offering a growing pool of certifications to meet this demand.
  • Enterprise growth. The company is also signing large-scale deployments with companies to be their internal learning platform. The company currently has just shy of 1.7k enterprise customers, up 12% y/y.

To me, however, one of the biggest advantages to Coursera remains its undemanding valuation—even after its post-earnings spike. At current share prices in the mid-$11s, Coursera trades at a market cap of $1.84 billion. After we net off the $775.1 million of cash (against zero debt) on Coursera's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $1.06 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY 2025, Coursera has boosted its full-year outlook to $738–$746 million (6–7% y/y growth), which is a material boost over a prior $720–$730 million (3–5% y/y) outlook, and considering Coursera clocked in at 10% growth in Q2, I'd say there's still further room for upside ahead. It's also expecting an 8.0% adjusted EBITDA margin at the midpoint, 100 bps higher from its prior viewpoint of 7.0% and representing $59.4 million in nominal terms.

This positions Coursera's valuation multiples at:

  • 1.4x EV/FY 2025 revenue
  • 17.8x EV/FY 2025 adjusted EBITDA

For a company that is just beginning to lean into its profitability (H1 2025 adjusted EBITDA grew at a 96% y/y pace), I'm more than happy to pay a high-teens multiple of adjusted EBITDA (as well as a ~1x multiple of revenue) for a company that still has plenty of economies of scale to benefit from as it continues to scale its paid learner base.

Stay long here and keep riding the post-earnings momentum higher.

Q2 Download

Let's now go through Coursera's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Coursera's revenue grew 10% y/y to $187.1 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $183.2 million (+8% y/y) by a two-point margin. Impressively, Coursera's revenue growth also accelerated four points versus just 6% y/y growth in Q1.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases the company's growth in registered learners, up 18% y/y to 183 million. The company added 8 million net-new learners in Q2: a good chunk of which was driven by the appeal of the company's new generative AI content, which as previously mentioned, is signing up a new enrollee at an average of every 12 minutes.

Impressively, Coursera has driven revenue growth acceleration in both its consumer and enterprise segments. The consumer segment delivered $122.8 million in revenue (roughly two-thirds of the company total), with growth accelerating to 10% y/y (from 5% y/y growth in Q1), driven by improved attach rates on the company's Coursera Plus subscriptions (which give paid users access to more courses and include certifications).

Meanwhile, the company also added 35 net-new enterprise customers, with enterprise revenue of $64.3 million increasing 10% y/y (representing 3 points of acceleration versus 7% growth in Q1).

On the profitability front, Coursera has spent slightly more on sales and marketing to chase growth, with sales and marketing spend rising to 31% of revenue, 2 points higher than the year-ago quarter. However, the company has offset this increase with a 2-point reduction in R&D spending. Overall, opex declined to 50% of revenue, one point lower than in the year-ago quarter.

Meanwhile, adjusted EBITDA surged 73% y/y to $18.0 million, or a 9.6% margin that improved 350bps y/y.

We note as well that Coursera is delivering tremendous growth in FCF, with first-half FCF of $53.9 million representing a 15% margin and growing 54% y/y.

Risks and Key Takeaways

In my view, the post-earnings surge in Coursera is a strong signal that investors are beginning to latch onto the company's growth story. Coursera is enjoying measurable tailwinds from AI courses and AI content translation, while a growing number of both individual consumers as well as enterprises are converting into paid subscription members. On top of a cash-rich and debt-free balance sheet with significant increases in operating and FCF margins, this is a great stock to hold onto as it rallies.

Of course, we should be cognizant of a number of risks that the company faces as well. The company is currently seeing very strong demand for its certification offerings for AI course content. This may be a short-term trend that doesn't produce sustainable growth if certifications prove to be insufficient indicators of AI proficiency. Also, we note that many software companies have reported weaker enterprise buying trends, which may stunt the company's progress in its enterprise division.

That being said, I see more upside than downside in Coursera's stock, especially at a very reasonable mid/high teens adjusted EBITDA multiple. Stay long here and keep riding the recent momentum higher.

data.title: Retire With Long-Term Investment Grade Bonds (Part 7): CMS Energy Corporation
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

Today's article is about another pick, with which you can retire. We talk about CMS Energy Corporation (NYSE:CMS) and its baby bonds.

Company Overview

Founded in the distant 1886, CMS Energy Corporation is an energy company headquartered in Jackson, Michigan, and that is focused principally on utility operations in Michigan. The total market capitalization of the company is close to $22 billion, and the total assets at the end of the first quarter of 2025 were around $36.2 billion.

Expansive Economic Development Efforts drive diversified growth across Michigan and reduce customer rates:

The company has shown good resilience to market volatility, with its shares up nearly 10% year-to-date, outperforming the utility indexes and even the S&P 500.

And here is a 10-year total return comparison with its peers:

Dividends

CMS has 17 consecutive years of uninterrupted and consistently growing dividend payments. Below, you can see the dividend history of the company:

For the past 12 months, CMS has paid $2.17 total dividend. The current price of the stock is $72.89, with a dividend yield of 2.90%. This is how the dividend yield looks for the past five years:

And the forward estimations:

Credit ratings

CMS is rated with a "BBB" credit rating by Fitch:

Baby bonds

CMS has three baby bonds:

All of them are rated with a credit rating "BBB-" by S&P and a credit rating "Baa3" by Moody's. All three are traded below par at the moment. All of them have passed their call dates, so they are all callable.

(CMSA) is with a current price of $21.69. It has 52.6 years to maturity, with a maturity date on 03/15/2078 and yield to maturity of 6.72%. It pays interest distributions of 5.625% per annum, $1.40625, in the form of $0.3515625 per quarter. CMS issued 8 million notes from CMSA on 03/05/2018.

At the time of writing, (NYSE:CMSD) is priced $22.89. It has a yield to maturity of 6.66%, and 53.6 years to maturity, with a maturity date on 03/01/2079. It pays interest distributions of 5.875% annually, or $1.47 per annum, in the form of $0.3671875 per quarter. CMS issued 25.2 million notes from CMSD on 02/06/2019.

(NYSE:CMSC) is with the highest yield to maturity from all three bonds: 6.75%. It has 53.23 years to maturity, with maturity date on 10/15/2078. Its current price is $22.43. CMSC pays interest distributions of 5.575% per annum, $1.47 annual amount, in the form of $0.3671875 quarterly. CMS issued 10 million notes from CMSC on 09/20/2018.

OTC bonds

In the chart below are the over-the-counter bonds of CMS:

Most of them are rated with an A1 credit rating by Moody's, but there are also ratings like Baa2, Baa3, and Ba2. Their yield range is from 4.25% to 6.10%.

Comparison with peers

Here is a comparison with other exchange-traded securities that are in the Electric sector, trade below par, have a long duration, and are investment grade:

CMS bonds, especially CMSC and CMSA, outperform most of their peers in the Electric sector by an average of about 0.5%, and some even by 1%.

Conclusion

CMS Energy baby bonds offer decent yields to maturity and outperform their peers in the sector. The securities are investment grade, trade below par, and provide stable fixed income, making them an attractive choice for investors seeking a more secure income with moderate risk and a long-term horizon.

data.title: Vallourec S.A. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Vallourec S.A. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: MGC: A Sharper, Leaner S&P 500 Built For The Long Game
data.description:
Investing in the Vanguard Mega Cap Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:MGC) is a question of how mega caps are poised to perform against the broader markets going ahead. Historically, mega caps have been known for lower risk, but in the past couple of years, it has been the growth epicenter in the US markets. With elevated valuations but long-term AI tailwinds that led the rally, are mega caps and MGC the new SPY or VOO? Let us dive deeper into MGC to understand that better.

Portfolio Characteristics

MGC is a SPY minus the lower market cap stocks. That is, MGC mostly shaves off the bottom ~320 names from the S&P 500 to still deliver broad market exposure, but through concentrated bets on the largest of large caps. How does that impact the sectoral allocations today? Not by much. Overall, the allocations are roughly retained, although the smaller stocks are removed. And that makes sense because SPY and most other market-weighted approaches hardly leave any weight for the bottom half of the holdings—tilting heavily towards larger caps. More so today, but even historically, I don't expect MGC's ~180 holdings to contribute less than 70% of the total 500 in SPY or VOO. I checked SPY's overall holdings as of today and saw ~80% in the top 150. So, sectoral exposure is retained in MGC. Slightly overweight in tech, financials, communications, and healthcare, but nothing significant to write home about.

The top 10 holdings also show minimal differences between MGC and S&P 500 ETFs. The concentration in the top 10 is still the same, although technically, MGC is allowed a concentration more than SPY because of its smaller portfolio size. All in all, MGC only removes the stocks that were serving window dressing purposes in SPY without losing much of its essence.

Performance

As expected, MGC hardly does anything different on total returns terms compared to SPY and VOO. Over 15 years and more, the differences are nothing structurally different. The minor outperformance in MGC is mostly concentrated in the 2017 mega cap run-up, exaggerated in the past couple of years.

The benefit of a smaller portfolio size and transactions is also not reflected significantly in the expenses of SPY versus MGC (0.09% versus 0.07%, respectively). Even dividend yields are roughly the same (1.04% for MGC versus 1.13% for SPY).

For long-term investors, though, even minor percentage point differences should matter. That is when we are talking about choosing between SPY and MGC. Assuming SPY is a long-term wealth builder (and I do have reservations against that), MGC could just pass on minor benefits for a buy and hold portfolio.

Outlook

There are broadly three scenarios in the immediate future for the US markets. In the event of a growth slowdown, MGC should hold up well because mega caps are overall more stable and have stronger balance sheets and cash flows. Until and unless the AI rally is threatened, that is. Tech valuation collapse is a horizontal scenario across all future paths, and MGC could face the brunt of multiple contraction more than SPY.

If rates are held higher for longer, I feel mega caps should still hold up reasonably better. More so because the tech rally is beyond duration risk and built around AI tailwinds working against high rates, as seen in the past couple of years. That story has no reason to change going ahead in the event of a status quo.

In the event of a rate cut, I see MGC still doing well, of course, from the growth tailwinds, but the rally will be more broad-based, and some cyclicals in SPY not in MGC could benefit from a broader rally—something like what we saw in 2020-21. Again, keep in mind, all we are talking about is a couple of percentage points here and there—no major deviations. The only risk to that thesis is when the broader markets (including small and mid caps that have underperformed for years now) see a boom and tech consolidates, bringing in large differentials between MGC and SPY—large enough for the missing 20% to dictate returns.

MGC is a More Refined SPY

Although tactical outlook may favor one over the other, MGC is mostly the quiet outperformer in comparison to SPY over the long term. A less than 0.5% CAGR difference between the two should not excite investors usually, but when we see the odds of earning the extra ~0.5% annually over the long term are quite high without disturbing the risk profile, there is no reason not to prefer MGC as a core portfolio holding, challenging SPY.

I rate MGC a buy for the long term. That mega caps are likely to continue to outperform is only an additional tactical edge. The switch from SPY to MGC should not even impact the risk profile of investors much. Below is the performance comparison in the prolonged downturn of 2022. MGC underperformed by only a percentage point during the period. That is the extent of risk at stake for a CAGR difference of ~0.35% over the long term.

data.title: BCAT: 22% Yield, But Falling Payouts
data.description:
Investors looking for a mix of high-yield equity and fixed income can utilize closed end funds to do the heavy lifting for them.

One of these CEFs is the BlackRock Capital Allocation Term Trust (NYSE:BCAT), which holds a mix of equity and fixed income, similar to some traditional figures for a balanced portfolio.

Fund Profile:

BCAT's investment objectives are to provide total return and income, through a combination of current income, current gains and long-term capital appreciation. The Trust invests in a portfolio of equity and debt securities. Generally, the Trust’s portfolio will include both equity and debt securities. At any given time, however, the Trust may emphasize either debt securities or equity securities. The Trust utilizes an option writing (selling) strategy in an effort to generate current gains from options premiums and to enhance the Trust’s risk-adjusted returns. (BCAT site)

BCAT has a 12-year limited term, subject to extension, with a contingent feature to convert to perpetual. Its inception date was 9/28/20.

BCAT is in the top 20 largest equity CEFs on a market cap basis. As of 6/30/25, it had 1,765 holdings, up significantly from the 865 it held at 9/30/24. The BCAT website lists 331% as the amount of its portfolio, which is overwritten by call options. It has an average daily volume of 555K, up 31% vs. 424K at 9/30/24. As of 6/30/25, its expense ratio was 1.47%, vs. 1.41% at 9/30/24.

Holdings:

BCAT's portfolio was split between equity, at 51.8%, fixed income, at 45.66%, and commodities, at 2.91%, as of 6/30/25. Its equity exposure is somewhat lower vs. the 55.43% it held as of 9/30/24.

Tech and financials remain the two highest sector exposures, representing 21.6% of the portfolio, as of 6/30/25. These top 10 sectors formed ~52% of BCAT's equity holdings, vs. ~55% at 9/30/24:

As of 6/30/25, BCAT's top 10 holdings represented just 14.7% of its portfolio. It still held 5 of the Magnificent 7 stocks - Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Nvidia (NVDA), Google (GOOG), and Broadcom (AVGO). However, Apple (AAPL) was no longer in the top 10 - it was replaced by Broadcom. Also gone were Mastercard (MA), United Healthcare (UNH), JPMorgan (JPM), and Progressive (PGR), which were replaced by Marsh & McLennan (MMC), BAE (OTCPK:BAESF), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), and Costco. TSM has benefited from AI demand - it makes advanced processors for Nvidia, for example - its revenue in Q2 '25 rose more than 38% vs. Q2 '24.

As of 6/30/25, BCAT's fixed income exposures represented ~15.6% of its portfolio. It was comprised of senior corporate notes, preferreds, private notes, and mortgage backed securities.

Management has cut back the fund's North American equity exposure, from 41% to 36%,; and its North American fixed income exposure, from 35.4% to 32.5%. That's in favor of increasing its exposure to Europe, with equities allocation up from 8.65% at 9/30/24 to 10.6%, and fixed income up from 7% to 10.13%. Japanese equities exposure fell from 2.5% to 1.2%, while emerging market equities ~doubled, rising from ~2% to ~4%:

BCAT's fixed income portfolio has ~16% in investment grade holdings, with ~13% in non-investment grade, and ~16% in unrated holdings:

Since 9/30/24, management has increased 0-5 year maturities from ~10% to ~20% and decreased 5-10 year maturities from ~26% to ~17%, with 10-20 years and 20-plus years remaining stable:

Dividends:

BCAT's most recent monthly distribution was $0.2755 - it went ex-dividend on 7/15/25 and will pay out on 7/31/25. At its intraday 7/23/25 price, BCAT yielded a whopping 22%.

After rising from $.2865 to $.2892 in Q3-4 '24, BCAT's monthly payouts have decreased 4.7% so far in 2025, falling from $.2892 to $.2755.

NII fell 12.6% in 2024, to $51.2M, vs. $58.58M in 2023, whereas net realized gains swung to a $26.5M gain, vs. a $56.8M loss in 2023. Unrealized appreciation in 2024 was $116.67M, down vs. $204.7M in 2023.

NII and net gains covered 2024 distributions by just 0.28X in 2024, but that was an improvement vs. the negligible coverage factor in 2023. ~80% of 2024 distributions were estimated as coming from return of capital.

ROC offers investors a deferred tax advantage, but it does decrease your basis, which will affect your taxable profit if you sell your shares.

Taxes:

Management estimates that 91% of BCAT's 2025 distributions have come from ROC.

Performance:

BCAT has outperformed the Morningstar US CEF US Allocation category so far in 2025, and in 2023. It took a 32%-plus dive in 2022, a rate hike year.

Cumulatively, BCAT has outperformed its category over the past three- and six-month periods, while outperforming it on a price basis over the past three years, as of 6/30/25:

Risks:

ROC: BCAT's monthly distributions may consist of returns of capital, which would decrease the fund’s NAV and trading price over time.

Option execution: It's not a given that the managers of the fund will choose the most optimal price strikes all the time. Some trades will be losers.

Rising interest rates: While BCAT does have exposure to several sectors, its leading exposure is tech, at ~ 12%. As shown in the calendar year performance chart above, BCAT fell 32% in price and 14% in NAV in 2022, a rising rate year.

Valuations:

Buying a CEF at lower NAV premiums or deeper NAV discounts vs. its historical averages can be a useful strategy due to mean reversion. The NAV/share is calculated after the end of the trading day, and is listed the following day.

At its 7/22/25 closing price of $14.86, BCAT was selling at a 4.25% discount to NAV/share, a smaller discount than its one-year average discount of 5.21%, but a much smaller discount than its three-year average discount of 10.25%, due to its big dip in 2022.

Parting Thoughts:

We rate BCAT a Sell. The current discount to NAV is shallower than its three-year average discount. Its decreasing monthly distributions are also a negative.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: Cofinimmo SA 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Cofinimmo SA in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Italian Confidence Edges Higher As Modest Growth Path Holds Firm
data.description:
By Paolo Pizzoli

Consumers more upbeat in July

Italian consumer confidence improved in July, with most components posting monthly gains except expectations of future economic developments. Interestingly, this did not impact expectations of future unemployment, which remained stable at the June level, confirming past declines. A steady belief in labour market resilience is reflected in improved expectations of household balance sheets and rising intentions of purchasing durable goods. If confirmed, these indications might point to stronger consumption over the summer months. Interestingly, this seems consistent with the improvement in confidence among retailers, driven by better sales expectations.

Signs of improvement in the manufacturing domain confirmed in July

The picture is also slowly improving among manufacturers. Here, confidence saw a fourth consecutive monthly gain, with a slight decline in inventories and a timid increase in orders, mirrored in improving production expectations and possibly hinting at the start of an inventory cycle. This seems more evident among producers of investment goods, who are reaping the benefits of recovery and resilience fund spending. Despite the lingering uncertainty related to the US tariff saga, the stabilisation of manufacturing is now morphing into a slow prospective recovery, which will presumably be fuelled by stronger demand originating from the substantial German investment plan.

Construction confidence softer, with mixed signals from components

On a less positive note, confidence had a modest setback in the construction sector. Unsurprisingly, the fall was mainly driven by a decline in the residential construction component, which had proved surprisingly resilient over the past few months despite the phasing out of the generous Superbonus tax incentive. The infrastructural component is holding up well, though, fuelled by the implementation of investments funded by EU recovery funds.

Confidence softer also in services, within a volatile downward path

Confidence data came in on the weak side among market service providers, with the exception of tourism services. Confidence in the service sector has been somewhat volatile over the last few months, with a downward trend suggesting that the sector cannot indefinitely bear the brunt of economic growth in isolation. Tourism itself is showing some signs of fatigue, particularly in the domestic component.

July confidence data still consistent with low but positive GDP growth in the third quarter

All in all, today’s confidence data releases point to a continuation of the low growth path for the Italian economy in the third quarter. Consumption might in principle play a bigger role, given labour market resilience, decent wage growth, and sub-2% inflation. The investment front will still reflect an infrastructural push and a residential drag, with developments in the machinery part very much linked to the end point of the tariff saga.

Should this prove bearable, the reduction in uncertainty would likely translate into a recovery in this component over the last part of 2025. We still believe that the Italian economy will post only marginal growth rates over the second and third quarters and accelerate slightly in the fourth, but we also acknowledge an upward risk emerging if a trade deal with the US is reached soon.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Intel: Turnaround Risks Vs. Upside Potential
data.description:
Intel Corporation (INTC) reported weaker-than-expected earnings for its second fiscal quarter as the chipmaker was hit again once again by restructuring costs and weighed down by a slow-moving reorganization. Intel also generated no revenue growth in the second quarter, on a year-over-year basis, and submitted a disappointing outlook for the third quarter with EPS. Intel also announced that it was seeking to cut more jobs and also scale back some projects in Europe.

Investors did not like Intel's results, nor its outlook for Q3, causing shares to skid 5% in the after-hours market. Intel is going to remain a restructuring play in the near term, but I see upside potential if the chipmaker can turn around its core operations and lower its losses in its core chipmaking unit.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of Intel a strong buy in April due to the possibility of a turnaround in its core business: 2025 Could Be A Rebound Year. Unfortunately, Intel’s financial situation did not improve much in the last quarter, due to a continual restructuring that is necessitating additional job cuts. However, as the company restructures and streamlines its operations, there is an opportunity for Intel to lower its operating expenses and boost its profitability, especially if the chipmaker succeeds in narrowing its losses in the foundry business.

Short-Term Turnaround Risks Need To Be Balanced Against Long-Term Restructuring Upside

Intel's continual restructuring caused the chipmaker to drastically underperform EPS estimates: in the second quarter, Intel generated a loss of $0.10 per share, missing the consensus prediction by $0.11 per share. The top line was flat, year-over-year, at $12.9B, but beat the revenue consensus forecast by $1.02B.

In total, Intel generated $12.9B in revenue in the second quarter, showing no growth compared to the year-earlier period. The chipmaker's gross margins also dropped further, to 29.7% based off of non-GAAP figures, showing a sequential decline of 9.5 PP. The Client Computing Group, which is the biggest segment for Intel, representing about 61% of consolidated revenue, saw a 3% top line decrease compared to the year-earlier period.

Intel continued to deal with a major restructuring of its core operations in the second quarter that resulted in layoffs as well as other measures, such as the scrapping of projects in Europe and slowed construction for one of its chip plants in Ohio. The chipmaker previously said that it will lay 15% of its global workforce and seeks to bring its employee count down to 75,000 by the end of the current fiscal year. These strategic actions are meant to lower Intel's operating expenses to ~$17.0B annually and streamline operations. Intel plans on further lowering its operating expenses to $16.0B next year.

In the second quarter, Intel recognized $1.9B in restructuring expenses related to the layoff of workers around the world. This restructuring expense led to a negative non-GAAP EPS impact amounting to $0.20/share... which is why Intel missed second quarter earnings estimates.

Another headache for Intel, in addition to pressure on its top line as well as higher-than-expected restructuring expenses, is the foundry business... which continues to lose a lot of money for the chipmaker. In the second quarter, Intel's chip-making unit generated $4.4B in total revenue, showing $100M Y/Y growth, but it is still losing too much money: Intel lost $3.2B (in operating losses) on $4.4B in revenue, highlighting a deeply loss-making business unit.

For Intel to succeed here, the chipmaker either needs to sign a big deal with other chip companies for the production of AI chips, or seek out a strategic (joint venture) solution with one of its rivals, such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM). TSMC this year announced a massive $200B investment program into the United States, so the Taiwanese foundry is definitely a company that I could see as a strategic partner for Intel going forward.

Intel’s Outlook

The outlook for the third quarter was a disappointment and likely explains why shares slid 5% after the Q2 earnings report card. The company expects breakeven EPS and a gross margin of 36%, which implies a 6 PP margin improvement Q/Q, on a non-GAAP basis. Therefore, with the outlook incorporating the expectation of a sequential margin rebound, I believe that investors responded a bit too negatively, and I continue to see shares of Intel as a strong buy.

Intel’s Valuation

Intel is currently trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 28.8X, which is a high valuation multiplier for a company in a restructuring. However, as the company restructures and focuses on its core operations, such as the Client Computing Group and Data Centers, lowers operating expenses, and rationalizes its CapEx spend, Intel has an opportunity to boost its EPS growth potential. Further, I continue to expect Intel to offer up a strategic solution for its chip-making unit, which could help the company narrow its operating losses.

Compared to chipmakers Nvidia (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), which are crushing it right now in the GPU and AI accelerator market, Intel is sitting idly on the sidelines and focused on cutting costs, so it is easy to see why Intel's share price is languishing and Nvidia's reached a new all-time high lately.

Nvidia is currently the most valuable company in the world with a market cap exceeding $4 trillion and shares are valued at a P/E ratio, on a forward FY 2026 basis, of 29.8X. I wrote about the potential for accelerating CapEx spending in my work on Nvidia -- Why I Am Accumulating Nvidia At All-Time Highs -- and continue to see the chip-design company as fundamentally undervalued.

AMD is trading at a 28.0X P/E ratio and has a ton of catalysts in its business, most of which relate to growing AI spend and an increasingly filled AI accelerator pipeline: Avalanche Of Catalysts. Additionally, both Nvidia and AMD received a boost from federal regulators which have greenlighted the resumption of chip exports to China.

While Intel's stock seems comparatively expensive, given its restructuring backdrop, Intel has potential to grow its EPS on the back of a rebound in the Client Computing Group, operating expense cuts and, potentially, a joint venture announcement with regard to its foundry operations. In my last work on Intel, I calculated a fair value of $33 per share for the chipmaker based off of historical P/E ratios. I believe this price target will be hard to achieve for Intel in the next twelve months as it continues to work through its restructuring, but it is still a viable long-term stock price target, in my opinion, if the restructuring ultimately is successful.

Risks With Intel

Intel is currently focused on restructuring of its core operations that included the sale of a majority position in Altera, Intel's autonomous vehicle technology business. This transaction is expected to close in the third quarter. If Intel’s restructuring turns out to be successful and the company returns to Client Computing Group-driven top line and earnings growth, I see potential for a stock repricing. However, if Intel fails to reposition its business or lower its operating expenses, shares may languish for a very long time.

Final Thoughts

Intel's second quarter earnings report card was not great, and the outlook for the third quarter was a disappointment as well in terms of EPS. However, the gross margin outlook indicates that the chipmaker expects a rebound here in the short term, which could indicate a stabilization of Intel's business and could help drive a change in sentiment. Further, I fully expect Intel to present investors with a strategic solution to its problems in its chipmaking unit (beyond just mere cost cuts and delayed capital projects) as it loses way too much money on its foundry business in relation to the revenues this business brings in.

In my opinion, the most likely solution, is a potential joint venture agreement with another foundry, like TSMC, in order to share operating and development costs for new AI chips that could be fully manufactured in the U.S. in the future. While Intel's stock is currently out-of-favor, a successful restructuring and a long-term foundry partnership deal with another large-scale chip manufacturer could change investor sentiment and yield attractive long-term returns for investors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brad Simpson On Whether The Record Run For Markets Can Continue
data.description:
Markets continue to make record highs, but can that momentum continue with a U.S. trade deadline looming? Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist with TD Wealth, discusses how investors are grappling with tariffs, the latest corporate earnings, and ongoing uncertainty.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - We've got markets at record highs, heading into another US trade deadline. We've got a lot of positive momentum in equities, but is it going to be tested? Brad Simpson, chief wealth strategist, TD Wealth, joins us now with more. Brad, great to have you here.

Brad Simpson - Greg, it's great to be here.

Greg Bonnell - You brought some pictures for us and some charts to help tell the story. And as we laid it out, looking at markets, pushing on a daily basis, it feels like now into new highs, we've still got a lot to worry about. Are markets decoupled from the economic reality?

Brad Simpson - That markets might ultimately be decoupled from reality. So, I guess, right off the bat, I know usually we have some pleasantries, and then we go into a chart. But I think this really is a great way to start this. In about a week or so, we're going to be coming to the deadline for tariffs. And of course, that was supposed to be one of the big risks that are out there. And what we're looking at here is the direction of the S&P 500.

The arrow that you see going down, it's actually inverted. So what we're seeing there is trade policy risk and tariff risk is actually headed up. And if some of the concern out there is about tariffs and somehow on that August 1, because we've sort of seen along the way some big drops, this is a market that's not terribly afraid or worried about tariffs at this point. Now, there are some good reasons for that, I think, right out of the gate.

Mansi Desai, who's a senior analyst on my team, published a paper called Made in the USA about, I think, 10 days ago. You can find it on my LinkedIn, and it's a fantastic article, where she breaks down US trade policy but really makes, I think, a really convincing case that the US underestimated the strength of the folks they were going to be negotiating with. And particularly in China, which there's a long way to go to be able to negotiate this. But she makes, I think, a really brilliant point to say that America didn't hold or doesn't hold the cards in the big stick that they thought in these negotiations. And so what we're seeing, as we saw, the agreement of 15% with Japan last night that there's a better tone around trade now. And that better trade, ultimately, is, I think, them coming to the conclusion, yeah, they've got a strong negotiating position, but they didn't have the negotiating position that they thought they did.

And so we, right now and what we've had since our last quarter, was -- said, look, we think it's going to be somewhere between 15% and 20%. That's our 45% base case scenario for tariffs. That's certainly where they're starting to come in. And I think that's -- out of the gate, I think that's what we're looking at.

Now, to the second part of that question and your comments on the way in, is I kind of laughed to myself in your introduction. When part of your introduction is talking about the strength of meme stocks, that's usually, we better get our chief strategist out.

Greg Bonnell - What's going on?

Brad Simpson - What's going on, right? And so I think a little bit is that I want to draw a picture for you and folks who are in today and go, okay, so let's look at this picture and then what conclusions would you come to from it? So we start out of the gate right now, S&P 500. We know that there's a V-shaped recovery that we've seen, a rally that we've seen, again, has been focused on big-name and tech and tech companies, and now sitting at highs that we have -- highs that we've seen of concentration of these big-name tech stocks of roughly around 33%, 34% in the S&P 500. So let's call that we've eclipsed the peak of what we saw for concentration back in 1999.

And of course, we know a little bit what happened from there. But if you look at the size of this, let's call it somewhere between $18 trillion and $20 trillion in market cap. That means one subsector of the S&P 500 now is larger than any other nation's index. Just stop and let that soak in a little bit. So there's point one. Let's set that in a little bit, right? There you go, okay, when we're publishing our portfolio strategy quarterly for next quarter -- or for next quarter, coming up next week, and it's called Still Here. And why did we call it Still Here?

It's this idea of a year and a half ago, we introduced this concept of gray rhino. And a gray rhino, of course, is risks that we know that are there, but you can see them coming, but you do nothing about. So one we made the argument was this idea of deglobalization. And this is something -- and this is a little bit what we're managing through right now. The other one was AI. What is this going to mean? The positives and the negatives to them.

And this movement we're seeing is, if you want to look at what's making tech things move, it always comes down to, well, it's AI. Well, it's AI. Well, it's AI. And you've got to be careful with monocausal reasons when you underline those. The third one we underlined is war. And of course, we've got three pretty actively engaged theaters of war going on right now. Now, interestingly, these risks, the market has said, well, they're off to the side right now. And I think they are cooler than what they were. But we got to remember, they're kind of out there still, right? So we look at those and you go, the VIX is at 16 right now, long-dated puts, okay? If I wanted to go and I wanted to buy insurance on the protection of my portfolio, are dirt cheap right now.

So which means that the market is saying that I don't need to worry about it. I don't need to think of--

Greg Bonnell - Are spreads another part of that story? I know you have a picture about spreads right now being historic. So you're talking about relatively risk-free debt, compared to...

Brad Simpson - Yeah, so let's look at -- I brought a chart along for spreads. Let's look at this. So what we're looking at here is, so this is in fixed-income markets. And this is looking at the spreads between corporates from quality corporates over to the high-yield stuff and looking at it over treasuries.

Now, the one bang in the middle there is that the spread of corps over a 10-year Treasury is at the 0 percentile. So you see every one of those little black diamonds there, all at the bottom of every one of those bars? What that says is that, when you're looking at the spreads for corporate debt right now, from quality to junk, is at the absolute bottom, meaning it's the fixed -- it's the non-government side of the fixed-income market saying there's no risk. So we check that one off.

Okay, I brought another slide. And let's take a look at this. This one we're looking at factor exposure. So often, when we're talking about when you're allocating a portfolio, asset allocation isn't enough. You also want to think about factor exposure, factors like the DNA of what makes an equity company.

So if we can put up the factor chart I brought and take a look at this, what we can see is that -- no, not that one.

Greg Bonnell - Growth and higher risk, growth...

Brad Simpson - No. Nope. We're going to come to that one. That's the -- there we go. So let's look at this. This is looking at first half of 2024 performance of equity markets, but through a factor lens. So the DNA of what makes the equity market roll, what's driving it?

Well, momentum up 12%. Growth up 13%. There rest, low volatility, value, small cap, all in the negative. So the one fundamental -- it's like inside of your body, if you had one driver making yourself go, that's the one that's making it go. Okay, let's look at the next one. It's the last one. And again, the idea here is that, let's step back and just look at the world through a different lens and just think about this a little. So what we're looking at here is the S&P 500 move, which is the black line. The Renaissance IPO Index. So what were them there is, it's just the most speculative part of the market that you want to consider. And money is plowing into there. The top green one there is the SPAC Index. The SPAC Index is just the ability to go out and accumulate flow that are going to go into SPACs. How many people have been talking SPACs here on this show over the last couple of years?

Greg Bonnell - We've not been talking SPACs very much at all.

Brad Simpson - Right? You don't even hear SPACs. So these were absolutely dead, left at the side of the road. And where we are looking at today is, all of a sudden, this is back in vogue again. And my point with all of this is you go one way or the other and that -- and again, to bring it up, we're also talking about meme stocks once again. There's lots of money in the system. There's really, really, really -- it's really, really quiet with folks at work moving assets right now. In the equity market, if you're moving into the second half, what we saw in the first half -- and we wrote a little bit about this -- is that once we went after Liberation Day, retail or wealth clients really started to move into the back end of these tech names, and managers who've been underperforming the benchmark for the last couple of years, start looking down and say, wait a minute, I might underperform again this year as well. So they've all started to move into this.

So the last one in my picture of this is when the Joes and the Pros both are plowing into something, and these are all the steps, I'm not trying to lead us to an alarming ending here, but what I do want to do is a little bit is to say that let's think about this a little bit.

And if you had a chance to say, for my speculative money, this is what this market is. For my money that I'm investing based on my wealth plan, where I want to grow and I want to protect, and I'm thinking about funding things in my liability and things that down the road I want to fund, I think this is a place where you want to stop a little bit and make sure that there's a strong barrier between these two, that says how I'm going to allocate over here, where I'm going to speculate here.

But know that when we turn the tap back on a little bit -- and we can talk about one of the things that I think might be the thing that cracked this thing a little -- that we don't get caught up in this. And I think we're seeing some of that right now.

Greg Bonnell - Okay, we got about a minute. So you just teased people with what could crack it. Give us a little taste.

Brad Simpson - Well, so I mean, I think the long story short is that, in our publication next week, what we do is we do a deep dive into the US labor market. And why is that important? We know that ultimately the US economy is driven by the consumer. The consumer feels good when they have their job.

When we look at the labor market, it's very, very good in the United States. However, we have seen the last nonfarm payrolls come out, and they were starting to turn over. And when we're seeing adjustments to those nonfarm payrolls, we see them adjusted and we see them headed downwards.

When we take the consumer part of that labor part and look at the health of the consumer, what we can see in active data now, what we can see is both in restaurants and entertainment, we start to see those starting to roll over. When we split the high-income earners, low-income earners, low-income earners threw in the towel about 18 months ago. High-income earners are starting to really slow as well, I think.

And so what we want to go is to say that -- you and I have talked a lot about labor and consumer on these before, but I think in this one for us is that it's not that they're saying the end is nigh, but there is something in this labor market that is telling us that the growth in the United States is slowing. And it's not an inevitable recession, but we think that these are one of the things that could provide a crack as we move when markets turn back up again, when volumes turn back on and we enter into September.

Original Post
